observations.summary: Repository-universe was used only to confirm packet scope and path existence; it is not included as graph evidence.
Both launcher scripts converge on the same runtime targets and honor the same dotenv-suppression and recovery-mode environment controls.
The root runtime stack is layered as policy -> manifest/config -> OS launchers -> `src/main.tsx` -> `setup()` orchestration.
Workflow HTTP entry points are split between session-scoped state/report routes in src/server/api/sessions.ts and global template management routes in src/server/api/workflowTemplates.ts.
The router treats workflow templates as a top-level /api/workflows/templates surface, while workflow execution stays nested under /api/sessions/:id/workflow/*.

Final report persistence is explicitly gated by completed workflow state and immutable session-scoped artifact pointers; corrupt or missing reports are surfaced as recoverable read states instead of silent regeneration.
WorkflowRuntimeService behavior was inferred from its contract tests because the packet assigned the runtime test file but not the implementation file itself.
Most other assigned server test files in this packet were not on the traced workflow execution path and are therefore accounted for as inventory-only rather than graph evidence.
Workflow entry is opt-in from EmptySession. Default dialogue creation remains unchanged until the user opens the Workflows tool and explicitly selects a template.
Workflow template administration is isolated under a dedicated Settings tab and does not reuse the general/H5 settings sections.
Template editing is server-validated before mutation: the editor runs local required-field checks, then calls sessionsApi.validateWorkflowTemplate before create/update.
Invalid workflow templates are intentionally visible as diagnostics in both launch and management surfaces, but they are not rendered as startable/selectable workflow buttons.
Runtime workflow UI is split between read-only status/history rendering and command emission; transition controls emit structured commands rather than mutating workflow artifacts locally.
Navigation into the Workflows tab is store-driven through uiStore.pendingSettingsTab, allowing slash-command or other surfaces to route users directly into workflow settings.
Workflow API surface is centralized in sessionsApi and typed through desktop/src/types/session.ts, including template import/export, linked-start, report retrieval, and transition request contracts.
Primary execution path traced: src/tools.ts -> AgentTool.call() -> runAgent() or task registration, with swarm and compaction subsystems attached as side paths.
Backgrounded local agents and backgrounded main-session queries both rely on task-backed transcript output files rather than direct foreground stream ownership.
In-process teammate execution is not a separate agent runtime; it wraps the shared runAgent() loop inside AsyncLocalStorage-backed teammate context and AppState task state.
Permission escalation inside in-process swarms first prefers the leader ToolUseConfirm queue and only falls back to mailbox/file synchronization when the bridge is unavailable.
Conversation compaction is actively used by the in-process teammate loop before each turn when token estimates exceed the autocompact threshold.
reactiveCompact.ts in this lane is an autogenerated external-build stub, so graph evidence should treat it as a placeholder surface rather than a live implementation path.
Remote agent tasks are structurally separate from local_agent tasks: they persist sidecar metadata, restore across resume, and parse remote session logs for ultraplan/review outputs.
Teammate runtime selection explicitly clears inherited provider-managed environment variables when a provider/model override is supplied, preventing credential bleed across teammates.
The desktop release lane ties tag/app version to a matching release-notes filename before publishing.
The docs publishing surface uses npm ci and Node 22, which is operationally distinct from Bun-based project workflows.
The native desktop runtime bundles updater plugin support and explicit sidecar/process lifecycle management inside the Tauri library.
The assigned docs corpus includes bilingual agent content, but only the English agent subsection was in this packet scope, not English desktop/channel docs.

All four IM adapter entrypoints follow the same control loop: authorize/pair user, resolve or create a Claude session through AdapterHttpClient, attach a WsBridge handler, then stream server messages back through platform-specific UX primitives.

Attachment handling is centralized in shared adapter/common modules: AttachmentStore stages downloaded media under ~/.claude/im-downloads, checkAttachmentLimit applies platform/API size guards, and ImageBlockWatcher converts assistant markdown images into outbound upload work.
WsBridge serializes server-message handlers per chatId with handlerChains, which reduces race risk when adapter handlers await outbound IM API calls.

The memory lane is feature-flag aware: memdir.ts imports growthbook gating and path/session helpers, while AutoDream layers a consolidation lock and forked agent execution on top of the same memory directory model.

The API lane is broad and risk-heavy in this packet: client/provider wrappers, error shaping, prompt cache diffing, files API, usage and quota fetchers, and retry logic all sit under src/services/api with analytics hooks nearby.
